interactions.summary: Jiang states the hurricane form of oceanic currents: once unleashed, historical forces do not negotiate; Ukraine, the Middle East, and current events are examples of destructive currents running their course.
A student asks what starts the hurricanes; Jiang answers that currents are always in motion, empires energize borderlands, and prediction requires finding where hurricanes are forming.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer; student words are not audible in the transcript, but Jiang repeats the question.

interactions.summary: He predicts additional hurricanes: East Asia involving Japan and South Korea, American civil war accelerating around 2028, and an eventual U.S.-Iran conflict dragging in the world.

After another student asks how long a hurricane continues, Jiang answers that it runs until it overwhelms empire or exhausts its energy, using Vikings, Normandy, Rus, England, and status pressure as examples.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer; student words are mostly not captured, but Jiang repeats the answer context.

speaker_notes.note: The transcript mostly captures Jiang repeating or answering classroom questions; student audio is not independently transcribed except through Jiang's phrasing.

claims.claim: Oceanic currents are natural historical forces that cannot be stopped by negotiation once they begin; they end only when they run out of energy.
claims.temporal_scope: Core model statement in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang predicts the Ukraine war will engulf Europe and the Middle East war will engulf the world.
He predicts the current world will be completely destroyed or transformed because oceanic currents destroy everything in their path once unleashed.
Hurricanes do not have a single start; currents are always in motion, and empire formation already begins energizing future borderland hurricanes.
Jiang predicts eventual East Asian conflict but says it will not be primarily a U.S.-China Taiwan Strait war; it will involve Japan and South Korea.
Jiang predicts America will eventually fight a civil war and that conflict will speed up around the heavily contested 2028 election.
The big scary hurricane is Iran: Jiang predicts the United States and Iran will eventually come into conflict and drag in the entire world.
Viking expansion illustrates the hurricane model: excess wealth, power, and status pressure pushed borderland people to colonize, trade, raid, and found new political orders.

signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the lecture's starkest image of impersonal historical force.

signature_moments.moment: Spoiler alert: things will not end well.
signature_moments.source_phrase: spoiler alert, things will not end well
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the apocalyptic force of Jiang's forecast.

signature_moments.moment: There is no start; hurricanes are always forming.
signature_moments.source_phrase: There's no start. They're always in motion.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It prevents the model from becoming a simple trigger theory.

signature_moments.moment: Empire building is status overflow, not simple resource hunger.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they have wealth, power, but they don't have enough status positions
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's final mechanism for expansion and conquest.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's metaphor for a matured historical current that runs through empires and borderlands until its energy is exhausted.
The Viking-linked trading settlements Jiang identifies as the beginning of Russia.
The land of the North people, used as an example of Viking borderland energy becoming a new political formation.

chronology_notes.note: The American civil-war forecast is explicitly tied to the still-future 2028 election from the source date of 2025-02-18.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang names Eva while answering; the exact student question is not captured, but his answer indicates a question about whether the hurricane continues until it overwhelms the empire.
